ideas.Intentionality: every mental act is dire:
**Intentionality** is Franz Brentano's most important and influential contribution to philosophy — his thesis that the defining, essential feature of **mental phenomena** is that they are always **directed at**, or **about**, an object. Reviving and reformulating a concept from medieval scholastic philosophy, Brentano made intentionality the key to distinguishing the mental from the physical, and in doing so he laid the foundation for **phenomenology** and profoundly shaped the modern philosophy of mind.

Brentano's celebrated formulation, in his *Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint* (1874), holds that "**every mental phenomenon is characterized by... the intentional (or mental) inexistence of an object**" — meaning that every act of consciousness has a *content*, is *directed toward* something, contains an object within itself. To perceive is to perceive *something*; to think is to think *of* something; to desire is to desire *some* object; to judge is to judge *that* something is the case; to love or hate is to love or hate *something*. This "aboutness" or object-directedness is present in every mental act and is unique to the mental.

The importance of the thesis is twofold. First, it provides a **criterion of the mental**: intentionality is the mark that distinguishes psychological phenomena from physical ones — physical things (a stone, a chemical reaction) are not *about* anything, whereas every mental state essentially is. This gives psychology its distinctive subject matter. Second, the notion opens up a whole program of investigating the structures of consciousness in terms of the relation between mental acts and their objects. Brentano's most famous student, **Edmund Husserl**, took intentionality as the cornerstone of phenomenology, and through him and others (Meinong, Heidegger, and later analytic philosophers of mind) the concept became one of the central and most fertile ideas in twentieth-century philosophy — the key problem of how thought can be "about" the world.
ideas.Descriptive vs. genetic psychology:
Franz Brentano drew an important and influential distinction between two kinds of **psychology**: **descriptive psychology** (which he also called "psychognosy" or, in a sense, phenomenology) and **genetic psychology**. This methodological distinction was central to his project of establishing psychology on a rigorous scientific footing, and it proved highly consequential for the development of **phenomenology** through his students.

**Genetic psychology** is concerned with the **causal** explanation of mental phenomena — with discovering the causes, conditions, and laws of the *coming-to-be* of mental states, including their physiological and physical basis. It asks how mental phenomena arise, how they succeed one another, what causes them; it is an *empirical, experimental* science in the ordinary sense, continuous with physiology and natural science, and its findings are causal and inductive.

**Descriptive psychology**, by contrast, is concerned not with causes but with the careful **analysis and classification** of the elements and structures of consciousness *as they are actually given* in experience. Its task is to describe exactly *what* mental phenomena there are, and to identify their basic constituents, their essential features, and the ways they can be combined — to give an exact, analytical account of the structure of the mind's contents and acts, independent of questions about their causal origin. Descriptive psychology aims at exact, even *a priori*, insights into the essential structures of consciousness, whereas genetic psychology yields only causal, empirical generalizations.

This distinction — the separation of the exact *description* of the structures of consciousness from the *causal explanation* of their occurrence — was of great importance. Brentano's descriptive psychology, with its careful analysis of mental acts and their intentional structure, was the direct precursor of **Edmund Husserl's phenomenology**, which took over and radicalized the project of a rigorous, descriptive science of the essential structures of consciousness. The distinction thus stands at the origin of one of the major movements of twentieth-century philosophy.
ideas.Inner perception:
"**Inner perception**" (*innere Wahrnehmung*) is a key concept in Franz Brentano's philosophy of mind and his account of self-knowledge — his theory of how we have direct, reliable, and even infallible awareness of our own **mental acts**. Brentano gave inner perception a foundational role in his empirical psychology and in his theory of knowledge, distinguishing it carefully from ordinary "inner observation" and from external perception.

Brentano held that all our knowledge of mental phenomena is ultimately grounded in **inner perception** — the immediate awareness that accompanies every conscious mental act, by which we are aware *of that very act* even as we perform it. When I hear a sound, for instance, I am simultaneously aware of my *hearing* of the sound; this awareness of the act itself is inner perception. Crucially, Brentano argued that inner perception is **self-evident and infallible**: it provides immediate, indubitable, error-free knowledge of the existence and character of our own present mental states. Unlike external perception (of physical objects), which is fallible and can deceive us, inner perception cannot be mistaken about the mental act it apprehends — it is the one domain of genuinely certain, evident knowledge. In this sense, Brentano provocatively held that, strictly speaking, only inner perception deserves the name of true "perception" at all.

Brentano carefully distinguished inner **perception** (*Wahrnehmung*) from inner **observation** (*Beobachtung*). We cannot *observe* our own mental acts in the way we scrutinize an external object — because the very attempt to turn our attention to a mental state and study it deliberately alters or destroys it (one cannot, for example, calmly *observe* one's own rage). Inner *perception*, by contrast, is the incidental, non-observational awareness that accompanies the act without being directed at it as a separate object. This subtle theory of self-awareness, grounding the certainty of psychological knowledge in the self-evidence of inner perception, was important both for Brentano's project of a scientific psychology and for later phenomenological accounts of self-consciousness.

works.Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint:
*Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint* (*Psychologie vom empirischen Standpunkt*, 1874) is Franz Brentano's masterwork and one of the most influential works in the philosophy of mind and psychology — the book in which he sought to establish **psychology as a rigorous empirical science** and in which he introduced his epoch-making concept of **intentionality**. Though Brentano wrote relatively little that he published, this work exerted an enormous influence, above all through his students, and stands at the origin of the phenomenological movement.

Brentano's aim was to place psychology on a secure scientific footing by clarifying its **subject matter** and **method**. His central and most famous achievement is his answer to the question: what distinguishes **mental phenomena** from **physical phenomena**? His answer is **intentionality** — every mental phenomenon is characterized by its **directedness toward an object**, its "aboutness." Every mental act (perceiving, judging, desiring, loving) is directed at, or contains within itself, an object, in a way that no merely physical phenomenon is. This "intentional inexistence of an object" is the essential mark of the mental, and it gives psychology its distinctive domain.

The work also develops Brentano's influential **classification of mental phenomena** into three basic kinds — **presentations** (the mere having of an object before the mind, the most basic act, presupposed by all the others), **judgments** (the acceptance or rejection of a presented content as true or false), and **phenomena of love and hate** (the emotions and acts of will, involving attraction or repulsion toward objects). It further sets out his views on **inner perception** as the source of self-evident knowledge of the mind, and on the proper empirical, descriptive method of psychology. Through its concept of intentionality and its careful descriptive analysis of consciousness, *Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint* directly inspired **Husserl's phenomenology** and shaped the work of Meinong, the Gestalt psychologists, and much of twentieth-century philosophy of mind.
